Name: Sagrada Família
Tips: Book online weeks ahead for August. Get Nativity Tower ticket (38 EUR). Go at 09:00 for west-window stained-glass light. Interior far more spectacular than exterior.

Name: Park Güell
Cost Range: 10 EUR monumental zone (under 7 free; outer park free)
Tips: Book 2-3 weeks ahead. Arrive at 09:30 opening for lowest crowds. Dragon Staircase + 86-column Hypostyle Hall are must-sees. Free outer park has great city views.
Family: Good — older kids and teens love it

Name: Casa Batlló
Tips: Book ahead. Rooftop dragon-spine tiles and bone-column main salon are extraordinary. Magic Nights evening option available. On the Manzana de la Discordia block.

Name: Casa Milà (La Pedrera)
Tips: Book ahead. The rooftop warrior chimneys are the attraction. Go late afternoon for best light. Evening concert La Pedrera de Nit is expensive but magical.

Name: Hospital de Sant Pau
Address: C/ de Sant Antoni Maria Claret 167 Eixample
Tips: World's largest Art Nouveau complex. Almost no queues in August. Far more photogenic than it sounds. Magnificent garden courtyards. UNESCO World Heritage.

Name: Barcelona Cathedral
Address: Pla de la Seu s/n El Gòtic
Cost Range: Free nave; ~7 EUR full access + roof
Tips: 13 white geese in the cloister — each represents Saint Eulàlia's 13 years. Paid slot gives roof access. Far less crowded than Sagrada Família. 13th century.

Name: Santa Maria del Mar
Address: Pl. de Santa Maria 1 El Born
Tips: Built by the entire neighbourhood in 54 years. Most beautiful Gothic interior in Barcelona — equal-height triple nave. Visit morning for rose window light. No queues.

Name: Picasso Museum
Tips: Five medieval palaces. Outstanding Picasso early-work collection. Free Thursday evening slot is excellent value. Book online.

Name: MNAC — National Art Museum
Tips: Romanesque art collection is world-class. The MNAC steps create the longest visual axis in Barcelona — twin towers down to the sea. Worth visiting for the building alone.

Name: Fundació Joan Miró
Tips: Bright surrealist museum on Montjuïc slopes. Miró's primary colours unusually accessible to children. Combine with Montjuïc Castle same afternoon.

Name: Montjuïc Castle
Tips: 17th-century fort with 360° harbour and city views. Take funicular from Paral·lel metro (free with T-Casual) then cable car to summit. Walk the ramparts.

Name: Barceloneta Beach
Tips: Iconic 5km city beach. Go 07:00-09:00 for golden hour with almost no tourists. Rebuilt for 1992 Olympics. Barceloneta neighbourhood behind beach has real character.

Name: Casa Vicens
Tips: Gaudí's vibrant first house — Moorish-inspired tile work revolutionary for 1885. Less crowded than the Eixample Gaudí houses. In Gràcia neighbourhood. Almost no queues.

Name: Palau Güell
Address: C/ Nou de la Rambla 3-5 El Raval
Tips: Gaudí's early opulent mansion. Central hall with parabolic dome is unlike anything else. Rooftop chimneys = early experiments that led to La Pedrera. Book online.

Name: Parc de la Ciutadella
Tips: 19th-century central park. Row boats on the lake. Find the Cascada monumental fountain (Gaudí contributed as student). Great relaxed morning before museums open.

Name: La Boqueria Market
Tips: Spectacular but touristy. Don't eat at front stalls. The back half (fishmongers butchers) is where locals shop. Go before 10:00. Better alternative: Mercat de Santa Caterina.

Name: Palau de la Música Catalana
Address: C/ del Palau de la Música 4-6 Sant Pere
Tips: Stained-glass modernist concert hall by Domènech i Montaner. UNESCO World Heritage. The main hall ceiling — inverted stained-glass dome — is one of the most beautiful architectural details in Europe. Book ahead.

Name: Bunkers del Carmel
Tips: THE best 360° panorama in Barcelona. Civil War anti-aircraft battery ruins. Bus 24 or V17 to El Coll then 15-min uphill walk. Magic at sunset. Bring a picnic.
Family: Yes — flat ruins to sit on

Name: Hotel Barcelona Raval Rooftop
Address: C/ de Joaquin Costa 28 El Raval
Tips: 360° views: Cathedral Sagrada Família port. Walk into hotel lobby take lift to floor 11 from 12:00. Nobody stops you (municipal law: all Barcelona rooftops are public). Almost no tourists know this exists.

Name: Plaça de Sant Felip Neri
Address: Pl. de Sant Felip Neri El Gòtic
Tips: Hidden Civil War square — bullet holes still visible in church walls from 1938 bombing. Hard to find. Completely peaceful and shaded. Extraordinary contrast with chaotic Gothic Quarter outside.

Name: El Call (Jewish Quarter)
Tips: One of best-preserved medieval Jewish quarters in Europe. Hebrew inscriptions on walls. Synagogue Major — possibly Europe's oldest (€3).

Name: Carrer de Petritxol
Tips: Barcelona's most charming pedestrian lane. Granjas (historic milk bars) serving thick Catalan hot chocolate and churros since 19th century. Perfect with children.

Name: Parc del Laberint d'Horta
Tips: Oldest garden in Barcelona (1791). The cypress maze is genuinely challenging — adults and kids both get properly lost. Almost no tourists.

Name: Monestir de Pedralbes
Tips: Medieval Benedictine monastery. Three-story Gothic cloister around a garden — extraordinary serenity. Active nuns still reside. Combine with upscale Sarrià neighbourhood walk.

Name: Torre Bellesguard
Tips: Gaudí's least-known major work — medieval Gothic manor blending with modernisme. Built on ruined 15th-century royal palace. Almost no queues ever. Medieval basement remains extraordinary.

Name: El Born Centre de Cultura
Tips: Excavated 18th-century city ruins under 19th-century iron market roof. Neighbourhood razed by Philip V after 1714 siege — rediscovered during renovation. A ghost-city under glass.

Name: Mercat de Santa Caterina
Address: Av. de Francesc Cambó 16 El Born
Tips: Far fewer tourists than Boqueria. Enric Miralles's spectacular undulating ceramic mosaic roof (2005). Locals actually shop here. One of the great pieces of contemporary architecture in the city.

Name: Mercat de Sant Antoni
Address: Rondas de Sant Antoni 1 Sant Antoni
Tips: Spectacular renovated 19th-century iron market. Local alternative to tourist Boqueria. Sunday morning: antique book market fills surrounding arcade.

Name: Casa Amatller
Tips: Part of the Manzana de la Discordia (Block of Discord) with Casa Batlló. Chocolate museum inside. Gothic and Flemish influences — completely different from Gaudí next door.

Name: Arc de Triomf
Address: Pg. de Lluís Companys Sant Pere
Tips: 1888 World's Fair triumphal arch on wide palm-lined promenade. Walk the promenade toward Ciutadella Park — popular evening walk for locals.

Name: Torre Glòries (The Cucumber)
Tips: Jean Nouvel's bullet-shaped LED skyscraper. 4500 light points create changing colour patterns at night. Spectacular from Poblenou at dusk.

Name: Rambla del Poblenou
Tips: Poblenou's own tree-lined promenade — completely local weekend farmers market zero tourists. The anti-Las Ramblas.

Name: Plaça del Sol
Tips: The living room of Gràcia — residents bring chairs and sit for hours. Grab beer from corner shop (local habit). Real neighbourhood life.

Name: Carrer de Verdi
Tips: Heart of bohemian Gràcia. Independent bakeries artisan shops indie cinema. Creative energy. Teens love it.

Name: Carretera de les Aigües
Tips: Flat walking/cycling trail along Collserola hillside with panoramic views. FGC to Peu del Funicular then walk up. City below Pyrenees above. Zero tourists.

Name: Tibidabo Amusement Park
Tips: Hilltop amusement park with retro rides and city + Pyrenees + sea views. FGC Tibidabo + funicular. Best views in the city from the summit church.
Family: Excellent — teens enjoy the funicular + views

Name: Montserrat
Tips: FGC R5 from Plaça Espanya + rack railway or cable car. Benedictine monastery + Black Madonna + L'Escolania boys choir Mon-Fri 13:00. Hiking to Sant Joan viewpoint. Buy honey and mató cheese.

Name: Sitges
Tips: Whitewashed old town. Church on the sea breakwater. Cau Ferrat museum. Beautiful beaches. Best day trip for beach + culture.

Name: Girona
Tips: 90-step Cathedral staircase (Game of Thrones). Widest Gothic nave in world. Best-preserved Jewish quarter in Europe. Brightly painted Onyar houses. Full city wall walk. Rocambolesc ice cream by Jordi Roca.

Name: Tarragona
Tips: 2nd-century Roman amphitheatre steps from the sea. Les Ferreres Aqueduct (Roman multi-tiered). Roman Circus underground vaults. Cathedral built over a Roman temple.

Name: Nova Icaria Beach
Tips: Calmer sheltered cove north of Barceloneta. Playgrounds nearby. Metro L4 Ciutadella-Vila Olímpica. Better for families than main Barceloneta beach.

Name: Bogatell Beach
Tips: Wider less crowded more local than Barceloneta. Metro L4 Poblenou + 15 min walk. Space + calm + local crowd.

Name: Mirador de Juan Sas
Tips: Sweeping city panorama including Sagrada Família and sea. 15-min walk from Park Güell upper entrance. Almost no signage = almost no tourists.

Name: Hotel Colón Terrace
Address: Av. de la Catedral 7 Gothic Quarter Barcelona
Tips: Enter Hotel Colón, take lift to rooftop terrace. Cathedral facade at eye level — the best photo angle of the Cathedral in the city. Low-moderate crowds.

Name: Pont del Bisbe
Address: Carrer del Bisbe Gothic Quarter Barcelona
Tips: Ornate neo-Gothic bridge (1928) over Carrer del Bisbe connecting two government palaces — one of the most photographed narrow lanes in Barcelona.

Name: Palo Alto Creative Space
Tips: Former industrial complex revitalised as creative hub. First weekend of month: artisan market. Former factory with bamboo, vines, garden paths. Best example of Poblenou's industrial revival.

Name: Illa de la Discòrdia
Tips: Three competing modernist houses on one block: Casa Lleó Morera (#35), Casa Amatller (#41), Casa Batlló (#43). Stand across the street and see all three at once — three different visions of the same commission from 1900.

Name: Mercat dels Encants
Tips: Europe's largest open-air flea market under a spectacular mirrored stainless-steel canopy. Mon, Wed, Fri, Sat 09:00–20:00. The reflective canopy alone is worth seeing.
Family: Good — treasure hunt for older kids

Name: Mar Bella Beach
Tips: Young, alternative beach in Poblenou — electric atmosphere, water sports, nudist section further along. Further north than Barceloneta but less crowded and more local.

Name: Cadaqués
Cost Range: ~€30 return by bus (3.5 hrs from Barcelona Nord)
Tips: Isolated brilliant-white fishing town Dalí called home. Casa Anita for fresh fish (communal tables, no menus). Cap de Creus national park — easternmost point of Spain. Early start required.
